metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile parent SC3 ('must not be surfaced') with 9022's precision ≥0.8 assertion: either (a) add a dedicated spot-check assertion in test_judge_precision_recall that every labeled related-but-distinct pair in the fixture returns relation='related_distinct' (not surfaced), separate from the aggregate precision metric, or (b) revise the parent SC3 to say 'precision ≥0.8' consistently.
findings.finding: Parent success criterion 3 states 'a related-but-distinct pair must not be surfaced' (an absolute requirement on the labeled fixture), but child 9022 (S6) asserts only precision ≥0.8 on its ≥12-pair fixture — allowing up to ~20% of surfaced items to be false positives, which could include labeled related-but-distinct pairs. This is a scope-narrowing SC-contradiction pattern: the child covers a weaker condition than the parent criterion specifies.
findings.location: child 9022-d3e3-e97f-47e3 — Acceptance Criteria ('Fixture test test_judge_precision_recall (≥12 labeled pairs) asserts precision ≥0.8') vs. parent Success Criterion 3
findings.suggested_fix: In 9022's test_judge_precision_recall, add an explicit loop: for each fixture pair labeled 'related_distinct', assert it is NOT in the judge's surfaced output (relation != duplicates/supersedes/depends_on). Keep the aggregate precision ≥0.8 assertion as a secondary guard. This makes the test satisfy both the aggregate metric and the absolute 'must not surface' requirement for explicitly labeled pairs.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the exact `category` value for `enrich.md` front-matter (choose from `review | verifier | transform | code | exploration`) so the executor has a decided value, not a discovery task.
findings.finding: The `enrich.md` prompt's `category` front-matter value must be picked from 'the prompt library's closed category set (do NOT invent one; pick an existing member)' but the plan never names the specific value to use. The closed set is confirmed in code as `["review", "verifier", "transform", "code", "exploration"]` — the executor must discover the right member by reading source.
findings.location: Design section / child story S1 scope (enrich.md prompt): 'a `category` value from the prompt library's closed category set (do NOT invent one; pick an existing member)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the plan: 'Use `category: transform`' (the digest extraction is a data-transform, not a review or verification step). Alternatively `exploration` if the intent is analytical; decide and state it explicitly.

findings.finding: Sibling tickets touch the same file(s) with no ordering edge.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a depends_on/blocks edge to serialize, or partition the file ownership.

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile e1f4's Scope section language to match its What section: replace 'atomic cert-critical-section' with 'SEPARATE append (non-atomic with cert)' so both sections say the same thing.
findings.finding: e1f4's Scope section describes the enqueue implementation as 'the atomic cert-critical-section in src/rebar/llm/plan_review/ certification path, using lock.acquire,' but its own What section explicitly states the enqueue is 'a SEPARATE append immediately after sign_plan_review returns' and 'Correctness does NOT depend on atomicity' — a direct internal contradiction that could cause a developer to implement an incorrect (over-)locked cert+enqueue path.
findings.location: child e1f4-d518-3240-472e — What section ('Enqueue placement (no false atomicity)') vs. Scope section ('Enqueue hook + the atomic cert-critical-section')
findings.suggested_fix: Change e1f4 Scope from 'Enqueue hook + the atomic cert-critical-section in src/rebar/llm/plan_review/ certification path, using lock.acquire' to 'Enqueue hook in src/rebar/llm/plan_review/ certification path: a SEPARATE append (non-atomic with cert — see What section); the lock.acquire pattern applies only to the CLAIM primitive in the drain, not to enqueue.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Collapse or cross-reference the Success Criteria and Acceptance Criteria sections to eliminate near-duplicate entries — either make SC the rationale/intent layer and AC the sole verifiable checklist, or explicitly mark SC as 'elaborated from AC item N' for each entry.
findings.finding: The Success Criteria section and Acceptance Criteria section are substantially near-duplicate: SC items 1–5 restate AC items 1–4 with slight wording variations (e.g. 'ready-to-run' vs 'copy-paste', 'store-wide step' phrasing). This redundancy risks silent divergence as child stories evolve — a future edit to one section may not be mirrored in the other, creating ambiguity about which is authoritative.
findings.location: Success Criteria section and Acceptance Criteria section (all items)
findings.suggested_fix: Keep Acceptance Criteria as the single verifiable checklist. Convert the Success Criteria section into a 'Rationale & Measurability' section that annotates each AC item with the 'why' and measurement approach (e.g. 'measured by S6 labeled fixture'), rather than restating the requirement in slightly different words.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace all codename references in e1f4 and 2d0f with ticket IDs: 'S4 (leggy-heedless-mosquito)' → 'S4 (c1de-d6a0-6cef-4135)', 'S1 (indecisive-callous-armyworm)' → 'S1 (ee3d-fc7b-e280-4cbb)', 'S2 (wispy-cheerless-tick)' → 'S2 (2d0f-19f2-dd15-4336)'.
findings.finding: e1f4 refers to S4 by the codename 'leggy-heedless-mosquito' and 2d0f uses the codename 'wispy-cheerless-tick' as a self-reference — codenames not present in the sibling roster (which uses ticket IDs). A developer implementing e1f4 who follows the cross-reference 'OWNED BY S4 (leggy-heedless-mosquito)' cannot resolve that to a ticket ID without out-of-band knowledge.
findings.location: child e1f4-d518-3240-472e — What section ('Defense-in-depth fallback...OWNED BY S4 (leggy-heedless-mosquito)'); child 2d0f-19f2-dd15-4336 — Dependencies section ('S2 (wispy-cheerless-tick)')
findings.suggested_fix: Do a global find-replace of codenames with ticket IDs across all seven children to make cross-references resolvable without out-of-band knowledge.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Assign a doc-update task (to an existing child or S7) that covers: (a) adding `overlap_enabled` and all `REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_*` tunables to the config reference doc, (b) documenting the advisory output format and the `rebar link` workflow users should follow when a suggestion is surfaced.
findings.finding: The plan introduces multiple new user-facing config fields and env vars (`overlap_enabled` on VerifyConfig; `REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_*` tunables on LLMConfig) and a new observable advisory output (≤3 `rebar link` suggestions surfaced during plan-review), but no child story or AC line mentions updating a config reference doc, CLI help text, or user-facing documentation describing the advisory output format and how to act on it. The ADR covers architecture only; user-facing config and output behavior is undocumented.
findings.location: Config placement section; AC lines 2 and 3; child stories S1–S7 (no story assigned to user-facing docs)
findings.suggested_fix: Extend S7's scope (or add an explicit AC line to the parent) to include: updating `docs/` config reference with `overlap_enabled` default/semantics and the full set of `REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_*` tunables; adding a short 'how to act on overlap suggestions' section (what the output looks like, when to run `rebar link`, when to dismiss).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define 'own graph' precisely in the Design section: state whether it means (a) the ticket itself only, (b) the ticket + direct parent/children edges, or (c) the full transitively-connected subgraph (all ancestors, descendants, and same-epic siblings), and align the AC wording to match.
findings.finding: The phrase 'excluding the query ticket's own graph' is used in both the Design section and the first AC bullet, but the plan never defines what 'own graph' means — parent epic only, direct children, full connected transitive subgraph, or something else. An executor implementing the exclusion filter cannot resolve this without stopping to ask.
findings.location: Design section ('excluding the query ticket's own graph') and AC line 1 ('every non-graph-excluded ticket')
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence: 'Own graph: the query ticket itself plus all nodes reachable via parent/child/depends_on/supersedes/duplicates edges in the stored link graph (i.e., the full connected component in the directed ticket graph).' Adjust AC to 'every ticket not reachable from the query ticket via stored link edges'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the intended default value for each LLM tunable in the Config placement section (e.g., K=20 matching the validated experiment, conf_threshold, surface_cap=3, max_doc_freq as a fraction of corpus size) so executors implementing S1 do not have to invent them.
findings.finding: The plan lists all LLM tunables (proposition bounds, BM25F weights, K, max_doc_freq, conf_threshold, surface_cap, soak, lease, drain mode/batch) as living on LLMConfig, but states no initial default values for any of them. An executor writing the config schema for S1 must invent these defaults with no guidance.
findings.location: Config placement section ('ALL other tunables... live on LLMConfig')
findings.suggested_fix: Add a defaults table: K=20, surface_cap=3 (matching the ≤3 AC), conf_threshold=0.7, max_doc_freq=0.5·N, BM25F field weights=(title:2.0, body:1.0, tags:1.5) — or whatever the experiment used — so the config schema can be written without ambiguity.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a numeric precision/recall threshold to the first Success Criterion and AC (e.g., 'recall@20 ≥ 0.875 and precision ≥ 0.80 on the S6 labeled fixture') so the criterion is objectively evaluable at PR merge time.
findings.finding: The first AC bullet ('Detects semantic overlap/duplication... beyond deterministic file-impact and manual links') and the first Success Criterion ('overlap is detected against every non-graph-excluded ticket... measured by the S6 labeled precision/recall fixture') both reference measurement via a fixture but state no minimum passing threshold for recall or precision. 'Detects' is not quantified — an executor cannot determine whether 60% recall passes or fails.
findings.location: Success Criteria bullet 1; AC bullet 1
findings.suggested_fix: Given the experiment showed recall@20 = 1.0 at N=200, a defensible threshold is recall@20 ≥ 0.875 (lower-bound of the N=8 experiment CI) and precision ≥ 0.80 on the labeled fixture. State both thresholds explicitly in the Success Criterion.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify explicitly that the enrichment-queue's read-check-append is a SINGLE locked critical section (read digest-present flag + check + append all under one `lock.acquire`), or describe the idempotency mechanism that makes duplicate enqueue entries safe (e.g. the drain deduplicates by ticket_id before extracting).
findings.finding: The enrichment-queue's read-check-append atomicity claim is not demonstrated to be TOCTOU-free. The plan asserts the manual write-lock 'supports the queue's read-check-append AND atomic enqueue' (citing txn.py precedent), but never states that all three operations (read the existing digest, check if it needs refresh, append the new enqueue event) occur WITHIN A SINGLE lock acquisition. If the digest cache is read outside the lock and the lock is acquired only for the append, two concurrent enrichment writers can both observe a stale/absent digest, both decide to enqueue, and both write — producing duplicate enrichment work or (worse) a double-extract race on the same ticket. The plan's own G6 criterion flags 'check-then-act idempotency that is really a TOCTOU race' — yet the enrichment enqueue mechanism is described without showing this race is closed.
findings.location: Design section — 'Store primitives: CONFIRMED. the manual write-lock (lock.acquire/LockHandle, txn.py precedent) supports the queue's read-check-append AND atomic enqueue'
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to the Design: 'The enqueue is idempotent — the drain deduplicates by ticket_id (last-write-wins on the digest), so a duplicate enqueue event causes at most one redundant LLM extraction, not a state corruption.' Alternatively, state that the read-present-check-append triple runs under a single lock acquisition (mirroring txn.py's re-read-under-lock pattern).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define 'own graph' precisely in the Design section: enumerate which link relation types (e.g. parent/child hierarchy + depends_on + duplicates/supersedes, but NOT relates_to) constitute graph membership and whether the boundary is direct-neighbors only or transitively closed.
findings.finding: The 'own graph' exclusion boundary is load-bearing but undefined. The plan uses 'excluding the query ticket's own graph' in the Design section and 'every non-graph-excluded ticket' in the first Success Criterion, but never specifies the boundary: parent epic only? direct parent + children? all siblings in the same epic? the full transitive closure across all link types (depends_on, relates_to, duplicates, supersedes, discovered_from, blocks)? The two extreme interpretations (ticket-only vs. full connected component) yield substantially different false-positive rates for intra-epic work, and the link vocabulary in the codebase (src/rebar/graph/_links.py:22) includes six relation types with different semantics.
findings.location: Design section — 'brute-force BM25F over corpus digests narrows to ~20 candidates, excluding the query ticket's own graph'; Success Criteria line 1 — 'every non-graph-excluded ticket in the store'
findings.suggested_fix: Add: 'Own graph: the query ticket itself, its direct parent, its direct children, and any ticket it depends_on, duplicates, or supersedes (one hop, directional). relates_to and discovered_from do NOT confer graph membership — they are informational and should not suppress overlap detection with the linked ticket.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the config-split rationale to state the ACTUAL distinguishing principle: behavioral tunables that control model/LLM/recall/cost behavior go on LLMConfig; gate-behavioral tunables that control whether/how-much the gate runs (thresholds, caps, enable flags) go on VerifyConfig — and verify that this is consistent with existing VerifyConfig fields (verify_window_headroom, novelty_drop_threshold, etc.).
findings.finding: The config-split rationale contains a factually incorrect premise about VerifyConfig's constraint. The plan states 'ALL other tunables (proposition bounds, BM25F weights, K, max_doc_freq, conf_threshold, surface_cap, soak, lease, drain mode/batch) are LLM-feature tunables and live on LLMConfig … tunables must NOT go on VerifyConfig.' The stated justification is '_config_schema.py reserves llm.* for that layer.' But the actual VerifyConfig in src/rebar/_config_schema.py:209 already carries multiple float/int tunables — verify_window_headroom:float (line 251), novelty_drop_threshold:float (line 270), novelty_priority_floor:float (line 271), remediation_window_minutes:int (line 263) — so the 'must NOT go on VerifyConfig' absolute rule is contradicted by the existing codebase. An implementer who reads both the plan and the code will find the rule inconsistent, with no guidance on which pattern to follow.
findings.location: Config placement section — 'ALL other tunables … are LLM-feature tunables and live on LLMConfig … tunables must NOT go on VerifyConfig'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'tunables must NOT go on VerifyConfig' with: 'Tunables that tune MODEL/LLM behavior (BM25F weights, K, max_doc_freq, drain batch, model choice) go on LLMConfig; tunables that tune GATE behavior (surface_cap, conf_threshold, soak) may go on VerifyConfig following the existing pattern (verify_window_headroom, novelty_drop_threshold), with the gate ENABLE flag overlap_enabled on VerifyConfig as the primary gate.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the fallback behavior when a ticket has no cached digest at query time: state whether unenriched tickets are (a) silently skipped (with the coverage metadata noting how many tickets were skipped), (b) synchronously extracted on-demand before the BM25F scan, or (c) cause the overall overlap advisory to note incomplete corpus coverage — and reconcile this with the Success Criterion's 'every non-graph-excluded ticket' claim.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify what happens at query time when a ticket's Cupid digest has not yet been enriched (i.e., the ticket is in the enrichment queue but not yet processed). The design says enrichment is 'async via an event-sourced queue drained opportunistically (Tier 1)' — meaning at any given plan-review invocation, some tickets in the store may have no digest. The BM25F stage needs a corpus of digests to search; a partially-built corpus silently degrades recall (unenriched tickets are invisible to BM25F). The plan does not state whether unenriched tickets are (a) silently skipped, (b) trigger a synchronous fallback extraction, or (c) cause the overlap check to emit an advisory noting incomplete coverage. This matters for the first Success Criterion ('overlap is detected against every non-graph-excluded ticket') — which cannot hold for unenriched tickets.
findings.location: Design section — 'Enrichment is async via an event-sourced queue drained opportunistically (Tier 1)'; Success Criteria line 1 — 'overlap is detected against every non-graph-excluded ticket in the store'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the library (or confirm a custom implementation) that delivers BM25F multi-field weighting for the corpus retrieval stage, and add it to the dependency list or scope; confirm the validated recall figures hold against the chosen implementation, not just against a conceptual BM25F.
findings.finding: The plan asserts BM25F (multi-field BM25) as its retrieval mechanism but names no library implementing it and adds no pip dependency. BM25F — unlike standard BM25 — requires per-field weighting and IDF calibration; no BM25 or BM25F library exists anywhere in the codebase or pyproject.toml. Either a library must be introduced (not named) or a custom implementation must be written (not called out as a scope item), leaving a load-bearing capability gap.
findings.location: Design section — 'brute-force BM25F over corpus digests narrows to ~20 candidates'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a note in the Design section (and the relevant child story) naming the chosen implementation — e.g. `rank-bm25` (note: provides BM25Okapi/BM25L/BM25Plus but NOT true BM25F field-weighting natively) or a stdlib-only custom implementation — and verify the experiment's 0.69ms/recall@20 numbers against that specific implementation rather than against the BM25F concept.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the Cupid digest schema (fields, types, approximate length) and the LLM prompt/extraction contract in the Design section (or delegate to a named child story with an explicit 'define the digest schema first' AC), so implementers can reproduce the validated recall@20 result.
findings.finding: The plan's central novel component — the 'Cupid digest' — is named but never specified: its schema, fields, generation prompt, and what properties make it superior to raw ticket text for BM25F retrieval are all absent from the plan. This is an unverified capability assertion ('LLM-extracted Cupid digest improves recall') where the mechanism behind the claimed improvement is opaque.
findings.location: Design section — 'each ticket gets a cached, LLM-extracted Cupid digest (no embeddings, no vector DB)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief prior-art comparison to LSH-based approaches (MinHash, simhash) in the Design rationale, explaining why the Cupid-digest+BM25F pipeline is justified over these established, enrichment-queue-free alternatives — especially given the no-embedding constraint already rules out the more complex dense retrieval approaches.
findings.finding: The plan does not consider MinHash-LSH or simhash — well-established, stdlib-friendly near-duplicate detection approaches that require no LLM enrichment queue and provide probabilistic deduplication guarantees for text corpora. These are established prior art for exactly the semantic overlap / ticket deduplication problem the plan targets, and their omission from the alternatives analysis is a T1(a) gap.
findings.location: Design section — 'Two-stage retrieve-then-rerank' and Context section justifying the approach
findings.suggested_fix: In the Design section, add a one-paragraph rationale such as: 'MinHash-LSH was considered but rejected because [reason — e.g., token-level hashing does not capture semantic paraphrase, which the digest+BM25F approach handles by normalizing intent into propositions before retrieval]. Simhash was similarly considered and rejected because [reason].'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly state the drain's resumability/idempotency contract: confirm that re-running a crashed drain skips already-digested tickets (e.g., via a 'digest sidecar already present → skip enqueue' check), so the one-time backfill is safe to restart without duplicate processing or cost overrun.
findings.finding: The plan does not explicitly assert that a partially-completed enrichment drain is resumable/idempotent if the drain process is interrupted mid-batch. The event-sourced queue design makes this structurally likely, but the plan only states the atomic enqueue is idempotent ('read-check-append AND atomic enqueue'); it does not state that re-running a crashed drain does not double-process or re-enqueue already-digested tickets.
findings.location: Design section: 'Enrichment is async via an event-sourced queue drained opportunistically (Tier 1)' and 'Store primitives: CONFIRMED. the manual write-lock (lock.acquire/LockHandle, txn.py precedent) supports the queue's read-check-append AND atomic enqueue'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence in the Design section: 'Drain idempotency: before enqueuing a ticket for enrichment, the queue checks whether a current-generation digest sidecar already exists (read-check); if so, the ticket is skipped. This makes the drain safely restartable after a crash with no duplicate digests or double-billing.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the specific pip package that provides BM25F (field-weighted BM25) and confirm its BM25F surface exists (e.g. `whoosh.scoring.BM25F`, a custom implementation, or an alternative); declare which pyproject.toml extra it belongs to and re-validate the 0.69 ms benchmark against that specific library.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'brute-force BM25F over corpus digests' as the retrieval stage and cites a concrete latency benchmark (0.69 ms at 800 digests), but names no pip package that provides BM25F. BM25F (field-weighted BM25) is not present in pyproject.toml or anywhere in the codebase. The dominant Python BM25 library (rank-bm25) provides BM25Okapi/BM25L/BM25Plus but NOT BM25F; the libraries that do provide BM25F (e.g. whoosh) are full search engines with additional overhead. The integration is therefore Unverified: the capability exists in the Python ecosystem but the specific library, its BM25F surface, and its extra/dependency placement are unnamed and unconfirmed in the plan.
findings.location: Design section — 'brute-force BM25F over corpus digests narrows to ~20 candidates'; Derisking experiments — 'brute-force BM25F single-query top-20 = 0.69 ms at 800 digests'
findings.suggested_fix: If a stdlib/zero-dependency BM25F implementation is intended (given the 'no vector DB' stance), state this explicitly and scope the implementation in the relevant child story. If a third-party library is used, name it, add it to the appropriate extra (e.g. [agents]), and confirm its BM25F variant exists via --help/docs.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the LLM pairwise judge's structured output schema in the Design section: specify the classification enum (e.g., `DUPLICATE | DEPENDS_ON | RELATES_TO | UNRELATED`), the confidence/score field the `conf_threshold` gates on, and the surfacing decision rule (`surface if classification != UNRELATED AND score >= conf_threshold`).
findings.finding: The LLM pairwise judge (Design stage 2) is described as classifying each candidate and surfacing ≤3 advisory suggestions, but its structured output schema — the verdict/classification enum values, the confidence field, and the field that `conf_threshold` gates on — is never defined anywhere in the plan.
findings.location: Design section: 'a bounded LLM pairwise judge classifies each candidate and surfaces ≤3 advisory link suggestions'; Config placement section: 'conf_threshold'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Judge output schema' subsection to Design: `{relation: 'duplicates'|'supersedes'|'depends_on'|'unrelated', confidence: float[0,1], rationale: str}`. Surfacing rule: `confidence >= conf_threshold AND relation != 'unrelated'`. Map to `rebar link` commands using the relation field directly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the `ticket_digest` schema in the Design section: enumerate its fields (at minimum: `ticket_id`, `propositions: list[str]`, and any flags/metadata used by BM25F weighting), so S1/S2/S5/S6 share a single structural contract.
findings.finding: The `ticket_digest` (Cupid digest) is the central artifact of the entire design — used by BM25F, cached, stored as a sidecar — but its field schema (what fields a digest has: proposition list, title tokens, flags, etc.) is never defined in the plan. Neither the prompt `enrich.md` nor the `outputs: ticket_digest` schema nor `register_contract('ticket_digest', …)` exist anywhere in the codebase.
findings.location: Design section: 'each ticket gets a cached, LLM-extracted Cupid digest'; Config placement section: 'proposition bounds'; referenced across S1–S7

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the fallback behavior when a ticket's digest is absent at overlap-step runtime: e.g., 'skip unenriched tickets and emit a `[overlap: N tickets lacked digest, skipped]` advisory note' or 'fall back to raw BM25 over ticket title+description for unenriched tickets'.
findings.finding: No fallback is specified for the case where the overlap step runs at plan-review time but a ticket's digest is absent (not yet drained from the enrichment queue) — a structurally predictable gap given the async 'Tier 1' drain model.
findings.location: Design section: 'Enrichment is async via an event-sourced queue drained opportunistically (Tier 1)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the judge call failure fallback: e.g., 'on LLMError/timeout, emit a single advisory note "overlap check unavailable (LLM error)" and return 0 suggestions; never propagate the exception to the plan-review caller'.
findings.finding: No fallback is specified for the LLM pairwise judge call failing (timeout, API outage, rate limit) during the plan-review window. Since the step is advisory-only, a safe default exists but is not stated, leaving the error-handling contract open for implementers to diverge.
findings.location: Design section stage (2); AC line 4: 'advisory-only; never blocking or altering the claim-gate verdict'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Design: 'Judge failure fallback: catch LLMError and all sub-exceptions; emit `advisory: overlap check unavailable ({error_type})` and return an empty suggestion list. Log at WARNING level for observability.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add explicit merge-sequencing notes (e.g., 'lands after 2d0f' / 'lands after e1f4') on all children that edit `event_append.py` EVENT_TYPES or `_version.py` _NON_REPLAY_KNOWN_TYPES to prevent silent merge-collision loss of event registrations.
findings.finding: Three children independently edit `src/rebar/_store/event_append.py` (2d0f adds TICKET_DIGEST to EVENT_TYPES; e1f4 adds ENQUEUE_ENRICH/CLAIM_ENRICH/DONE_ENRICH to EVENT_TYPES; c1de adds the maybe_drain hook) and two children independently edit `src/rebar/reducer/_version.py` `_NON_REPLAY_KNOWN_TYPES` (2d0f adds TICKET_DIGEST; e1f4 adds three queue types), yet no child specifies merge-sequencing between itself and the OTHER children editing these same files.
findings.location: child 2d0f-19f2-dd15-4336 (Scope), child e1f4-d518-3240-472e (Scope), child c1de-d6a0-6cef-4135 (Scope)
findings.suggested_fix: In e1f4's Dependencies section add 'NOTE: 2d0f also edits event_append.py and _version.py; e1f4 lands after 2d0f.' In c1de's Dependencies section add 'NOTE: 2d0f and e1f4 also edit event_append.py; c1de lands after both.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a per-process (or per-command-invocation) guard in c1de's maybe_drain design — e.g., a module-level `_drain_spawned` flag set on first spawn within a process lifetime, or move the call site to the top-level command dispatcher rather than event_append — and add a test asserting at-most-one spawn per command invocation with multiple event appends.
findings.finding: c1de places `maybe_drain()` inside `event_append.py` at 'the end of the write-command path' with intent that it fires 'once' per command invocation, but `event_append` may be called multiple times per command (e.g., txn.py appends multiple events in sequence). No single-per-invocation guard (e.g., a per-process flag or call-site guard in the top-level command dispatcher) is specified, leaving open the scenario where one rebar command spawns N detached drain processes.
findings.location: child c1de-d6a0-6cef-4135 — 'maybe_drain injection point (decided)' section and Scope
findings.suggested_fix: Set a module-level `_drain_spawned = False` flag in enrich_drain.py; `maybe_drain()` checks-and-sets atomically (within a single process, the GIL makes this safe) and no-ops on subsequent calls within the same process. Add `test_single_spawn_per_process` asserting that two sequential event_append calls yield at most one detached child.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
